source.title: Chemical Reaction, Decomposition, and Toxic Gas Release at Bio-Lab, Inc.

event.top_event: Loss of Containment - Toxic Chlorine Gas Release
event.incident_type: Chemical decomposition and fire resulting in toxic gas release
actions_taken: Shelter-in-place order issued for nearby community
Louisiana State Police closed portion of Interstate 10 at 9:54 a.m.
Bio-Lab employees obtained SCBAs for respiratory protection
Attempted to start rental backup generator to energize electric fire water pump
Manually started one diesel fire water pump at approximately 11:40 a.m.
Turned on fire water monitors to spray water on Plant 4 building
Discovered and stopped water leak in fire water system at adjacent Lonza facility
US Fire Pump third-party response resource engaged at 10:38 a.m.
Facility reconstruction completed and production resumed around March 2023
event.summary: On August 27, 2020, Category 4 Hurricane Laura caused severe damage to Bio-Lab's Lake Charles facility in Westlake, Louisiana. Extreme winds tore off portions of the roof of Plant 4, which stored approximately 70,000-100,000 pounds of TCCA-based formulation. Rainwater from the hurricane contacted the stored TCCA-based formulation, initiating a chemical reaction and decomposition that released toxic chlorine gas and caused a fire. The decomposition and fire destroyed Plant 4 and damaged additional structures. A massive plume of hazardous gases crossed the facility boundary and was visible over a large portion of the nearby community. The incident resulted in closure of Interstate 10 for over 28 hours and a shelter-in-place order for the surrounding area. Approximately $250 million was invested in reconstructing the damaged facility. No injuries were reported.
recommendations: Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Evaluate hazards from hurricanes and implement processes and safeguards for protection, including constructing buildings to withstand hurricane winds and flooding, implementing safeguards to ensure hazardous chemicals are not compromised during extreme weather, and following CCPS guidance on natural hazards assessment and planning
Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Develop and implement improved PHA action item management system with assignment, documentation, and tracking of all action items until resolution, and conduct periodic audits
Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Perform PHAs on all buildings and units processing or storing TCCA, revalidate at least every five years, and include building design basis as process safety information

Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Revise emergency response plan to require proper maintenance and function-testing of fire protection system, regular checking of emergency equipment one month before hurricane season, and training of site personnel on emergency generators and equipment

Louisiana Governor and State Legislature: Require existing chemical manufacturing and storage facilities in hurricane-prone regions to evaluate hazards from hurricanes and implement processes and safeguards for protection

OSHA: Amend PSM Standard to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards by broadening application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals, and augment PHA element to explicitly require evaluation of reactive hazards
EPA: Revise Accidental Release Prevention Requirements to explicitly cover catastrophic reactive hazards from self-reactive chemicals and combinations of chemicals and process-specific conditions
EPA: Implement five open recommendations from 2022 GAO Report on chemical accident prevention related to climate change and natural hazards risks

hazards.name: Inadequate building structural design for wind loads

threats.name: Inability to activate fire protection system due to power loss

consequences.name: Toxic gas plume crossing facility boundary
Extended facility downtime and business interruption

controls.name: Building structural design to withstand wind loads
controls.barrier_role: Prevent roof damage from hurricane winds
controls.lod_basis: Primary structural barrier to prevent water ingress

supporting_text: extreme winds from Category 4 Hurricane Laura caused severe damage to buildings storing chemical formulations
winds from Hurricane Laura caused portions of the roof of Bio-Lab Plant 4 to be torn off the building
The Bio-Lab Lake Charles facility, built in 1979, did not conform to the safeguards identified in the National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) 101 Life Safety Code

Bio-Lab's 2010 Process Hazard Analysis recommended the Lake Charles facility to 'consider evaluating warehouse roof structure for hurricane conditions; verify warehouse is built to withstand high winds'

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) and action item management
controls.barrier_role: Identify and manage hazards through systematic analysis and follow-up
controls.lod_basis: Systematic identification and management of process hazards

supporting_text: Bio-Lab's 2010 Process Hazard Analysis recommended the Lake Charles facility to 'consider evaluating warehouse roof structure for hurricane conditions; verify warehouse is built to withstand high winds,' but the company did not implement the recommendation
A lack of close-out documentation for the 2010 PHA recommendation indicates deficiencies in Bio-Lab's PHA action item management system
Bio-Lab did not implement industry guidance for extreme weather preparation that was updated and published after the Arkema incident

controls.name: Extreme weather preparation and planning
controls.barrier_role: Prepare facility and operations for extreme weather events
controls.lod_basis: Proactive preparation to prevent or mitigate extreme weather impacts

supporting_text: Bio-Lab did not learn the importance of preparing for extreme weather after the 2017 Arkema incident in Crosby, Texas
Bio-Lab did not implement industry guidance for extreme weather preparation that was updated and published after the Arkema incident
Bio-Lab was unprepared for the winds produced by Category 4 Hurricane Laura

Bio-Lab successfully transported about 825,000 pounds of TCCA-based formulation from the Finished Goods Warehouse to the Bio-Lab facility in Conyers, Georgia. However, two additional scheduled trucks never arrived at the facility, leaving over one million pounds of TCCA-based formulation product on-site

controls.name: Fire protection system - electric fire water pump
controls.barrier_role: Suppress fire and cool decomposing chemicals with water spray
controls.lod_basis: Active fire suppression system to control decomposition and fire

supporting_text: A backup generator was required to provide electrical power to the electric pumps during a power outage

One of the employees attempted to turn on a rental backup generator secured during the site's preparation for Hurricane Laura to energize the large electric fire water pump. Without this generator, there was no way to operate this pump since the facility did not have electrical power, and Bio-Lab's backup emergency generator was not functional
The employee, who was unfamiliar with the rental generator, was unable to start it
Bio-Lab did not ensure that enough of its staff, including its assigned hurricane crew, knew how to activate the rental backup generator

controls.name: Fire protection system - diesel fire water pump
controls.barrier_role: Provide backup fire suppression capability independent of electrical power
controls.lod_basis: Backup fire suppression system to operate during power outage

supporting_text: A wastewater treatment operator familiar with the fire protection equipment attempted to start the rental backup generator and energize the electric fire pump without success. He was, however, successful in manually starting one diesel fire water pump

By around 11:40 a.m., with the sole diesel fire water pump pressurizing the fire water system, the supervisor, two production operators, and the maintenance supervisor began turning on fire water monitors
The supervisor quickly realized there was inadequate water pressure at the monitor
Bio-Lab experienced serious delays in responding to the TCCA-based formulation decomposition and fire due to an inadequate and largely nonfunctional fire protection system

controls.name: Fire water system maintenance and testing
controls.barrier_role: Ensure fire protection system is functional and ready for emergency use
controls.lod_basis: Preventive maintenance to ensure barrier functionality

supporting_text: Bio-Lab did not adequately maintain its fire protection system to ensure its functionality
Bio-Lab experienced serious delays in responding to the TCCA-based formulation decomposition and fire due to an inadequate and largely nonfunctional fire protection system

Bio-Lab did not ensure that enough of its staff, including its assigned hurricane crew, knew how to activate the rental backup generator, which was necessary to operate electrically powered fire protection equipment during the power outage

controls.name: Automatic fire sprinkler system
controls.barrier_role: Automatically detect and suppress fire without manual intervention
controls.lod_basis: Automatic fire detection and suppression system

supporting_text: Bio-Lab did not conform to NFPA 400 Hazardous Materials Code, which required a fire detection system and an automatic fire sprinkler system

Had the Bio-Lab buildings involved in the incident been equipped with automatic extinguishing systems, it is likely that Bio-Lab would have begun applying large amounts of water to the decomposing TCCA-based formulation earlier in the incident
Contributing to the severity of the incident were Bio-Lab's inadequate and largely nonfunctional fire protection system and the absence of automatic extinguishing systems

controls.name: Regulatory compliance with NFPA codes
controls.barrier_role: Ensure facility design and equipment meet applicable fire safety standards
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement for hazardous materials facilities

supporting_text: The Bio-Lab Lake Charles facility, built in 1979, did not conform to the safeguards identified in the National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) 101 Life Safety Code for high-hazard industrial occupancies
Bio-Lab also did not conform to NFPA 400 Hazardous Materials Code, which required a fire detection system and an automatic fire sprinkler system

Existing facilities in Louisiana, such as Bio-Lab, are grandfathered to the state-adopted codes and standards at the time of construction unless changes are made or if the State Fire Marshal receives a complaint

controls.name: Regulatory coverage of reactive chemical hazards
controls.barrier_role: Ensure process safety management requirements apply to reactive chemicals
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory framework to mandate process safety management

supporting_text: Although TCCA and TCCA-based formulations can decompose and release toxic chlorine gas, TCCA and TCCA-based formulation are not regulated under the OSHA PSM Standard or the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Risk Management Program (RMP) Rule
As such, Bio-Lab was not required to implement baseline process safety management system elements to manage the safety of its TCCA-based formulation related operations under these regulations
OSHA and the EPA currently use predefined chemical lists to identify the processes subject to coverage under the PSM Standard and RMP Rule
The CSB's Reactive Hazard Study, published in 2002, concluded that solely using lists of chemicals is an inadequate approach for regulatory coverage of reactive hazards

controls.name: Emergency response plan and training
controls.barrier_role: Ensure rapid and effective response to emergency situations
controls.lod_basis: Preparedness and training for emergency response

supporting_text: Bio-Lab did not ensure that enough of its staff, including its assigned hurricane crew, knew how to activate the rental backup generator

The approximately five-and-a-half hour delay in responding to the decomposition likely led to an unnecessary increase in (1) the amount of TCCA-based formulation that decomposed, (2) the quantity of toxic chlorine released, and (3) the extent of the facility damage

Bio-Lab experienced serious delays in responding to the TCCA-based formulation decomposition and fire due to an inadequate and largely nonfunctional fire protection system and the absence of automated sprinkler systems

controls.name: Backup emergency generator functionality
controls.barrier_role: Provide electrical power to fire protection equipment during power outage
controls.lod_basis: Backup power system for critical safety equipment

supporting_text: Bio-Lab's backup emergency generator was not functional
Without this generator, there was no way to operate this pump since the facility did not have electrical power, and Bio-Lab's backup emergency generator was not functional
